speaker: interviewer
text: And so we have Eva Hoffman. December the 6th, 1995. Tape number five. You were saying that your father moved out and then you-- your husband was supporting you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Papa, Mama moved out, account of my husband was supporting me in that respect, that we had different lifestyles. Somehow they lived by the communist regime all those years. They had different values. So they moved. They took a private apartment. Then we went to help to paint it and clean it and furnish it for them. And they were very well. My father was taking on private jobs. He was a locksmith. And he had his chance to do things the way he wanted to do, but he was a jack of all trades. He was extremely handy to have around. And our relationship really improved after they moved out. We were visiting each other. They came by me, like, for Thanksgiving dinner or they came, like, for a holiday dinner. And-- and we went to visit them every week. So our relationship really got--

speaker: interviewer
text: Improved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Much improved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you didn't say that you'd like to say at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only things-- I am sorry about the people who I done wrong, which one I mentioned before. And another thing, which one had no bearing on the Holocaust, that I have very little knowledge of my father's family whatsoever, his background, his heritage. But this had nothing to do with the Holocaust. This is just my private shortcoming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you'd like for future generations or for your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I would like, they should try to keep a closer knit family and should have a little bit more interest in each other, each other's lives, not to be so self-centered, account of the years go by and the old ones die out. Like I lost my brother-in-law, my sister-in-law, and my oldest niece, and the years and the distances is really killing the relationships. They should try to be a little bit closer to each other. Sisters and brothers and cousins and all kind of slightly related relatives even, can't talk. This is the only way to keep in touch, with family closeness. If our generation goes, the next one gets even less knowledgeable-- whatever. I mean, everybody needs to have a-- a background, a backbone where they come from, good, bad, or indifferent. Rich or poor, but this had no bearing on it. We just have to try to do the best what we can. That's the only thing I could think of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mrs. Hoffman can you-- this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my lovely husband of 49 years, Edward Hoffman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met--

speaker: interviewee
text: We met in Sweden in 1945, and we got married in '46. And we are the grandparents of four lovely children. And we considered retired people, but we have a very pleasant and very happy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On the left side is my grandfather. His name is Frank Pollack. And on the right side is his wife, Eugena Pollack. And in the middle, you will find my mother and father vacationing on the Lake Balaton in Budapest. I would say the pictures were taken oh, maybe '47, '48. And my grandparents' pictures are maybe in the middle '30s or early-- latest '40, 1940. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] These are my parents on the Lake Balaton. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Klara and Karoly Deutsch. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Could be, I say, about '47. '47? Yeah, around 1947, in the summer on the Lake Balaton. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me. I would say I was about seven years old, six or seven, 1934, '35. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My name is Eva Pollock. And this was taken in Budapest. I-- I called myself Pollock after my grandparents since I lived with them. The name is Deutsch actually but I call myself Eva Pollock after my grandparents. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a family picture. On the left side is my uncle Laszlo with his wife of Elizabeth. In the middle, that short fellow is my grandmother's brother. His name was Baylor. And afterwards, I have on the right side, my Uncle Emre and his wife, I remember her name, but I think her name was Margaret, Margaret. The picture, I would say, about a '41, '42 production. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] The picture is of my son and daughter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The daughter is called Rossalyn Ellis [? Palacino, ?] previously Hoffman. And my son is Irving Hoffman. And the place is on my granddaughter's bat mitzvah, [INAUDIBLE] March the 19th, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is from Julianne's bat mitzvah. It's March 19, 1995. Edward Hoffman, his youngest granddaughter, Carly Hoffman, his older granddaughter Julianne Hoffman, and it's me, Eva Hoffman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to tell all the pictures? All the pictures? The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which two? This one is my daughter-in-law and my son. Her name is Bonnie Hoffman, previously were called Glock. Very lovely young lady. Comes from the bat mitzvah picture. And the other one is Rossalyn and Joseph [? Palacino. ?] It's, again, from the bat mitzvah. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] These are my grandchildren from my daughter's side. The name is Michael Jason and Nina Robin. Picture here, would say is from 1992. It's done in their house in Manhasset.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did say it already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said the name is Nina and Michael--

speaker: interviewee
text: Palacino.

speaker: interviewee
text: Palacino. I did say that.
